the open Development to the hidden Evaluation phase.

at72.73%72.73\%across top teams.

at72.73%72.73\%across top teams.

(b)Track 2 (Execution) public leaderboard. Low public–hidden
agreement.

Figure 4:Benchmark fingerprint.Normalised computational cost profile per agent domain
across five axes. Raw values and provenance is provided in Table15.

Figure 5:Track 1 (left) scatters with 32% noise; Track 2 (right)
forms three clusters with<<2% noise.

Figure 6:Archetype taxonomy atK=5K{=}5.Bar length: cluster share (%). Filled dots: medoids
stable across both encoders. Italic terms:
class-TF–IDF top tokens.

Figure 7:Alignment with the Agent-Eval
Checklist.
Green =
satisfied, amber = partial, red = gap.

header: Appendix ATechnical appendices and supplementary material

images.caption: Figure 9:Official Agentic-AI competition website. Link:https://sites.google.com/view/assetopsbench-challenge/home?authuser=0
Figure 10:Agentic AI Challenge Advertisement Webpage at CODS 2025. Link:https://ikdd.acm.org/cods-2025/data-challenge.php
Figure 11:Registration for for Agentic AI Challenge. Link:https://docs.google.com/forms/d/16L0f6ozrraTqu9_gUE6etoRCUsDTEOX62sfYEy7zmE8/viewform?edit_requested=true

Figure 12:Hierarchical cluster structure of observed failure modes.The tree is organized across three abstraction levels: a single root
node (left), seven failure mode clusters at the middle level (one
colour per cluster), and eight title variants at the leaf level (right).
Count badges above each cluster node show the total number of instances
belonging to that cluster.Lack of Final Answer(cluster 3, centre) is the only cluster
with two distinct title variants, reflecting two observationally
distinct manifestations of the same underlying incompletion failure.

Figure 13:Competition overview.(a)Score distributions per team split by track (blue =
Task Planning, orange = Task Execution).
White diamonds denote the per-team mean; medal icons identify the
top-three finishers by final combined score.
Task Planning exhibits wider variance (IQR up to 27 points for
high-submission teams) than Task Execution.(b)Best planning score vs. best execution score for each team.
Circles = both-track participants; upward triangles (△\triangle) =
planning-only; rightward triangles (⊳\triangleright) = execution-only.
The dashed diagonal marks equal performance across tracks.
Spearmanρ=0.41\rho=0.41(p=0.21p=0.21) indicates a weak non-significant
correlation between planning and execution best scores.(c)Total finished submission counts per team, stacked by track.
Team identifiers follow Table7.

Figure 14:Score trajectories over the competition window.Each dot is one finished submission; the step line shows the running
best score for each team.
Light grey dots in the background represent all submissions aggregated
across teams, providing a reference for the overall scoring landscape.(a)Task Planning track: most teams converge within the first
two weeks; Team A is a notable exception with a sustained ascending
trajectory reaching 72.73 in the final days.(b)Task Execution track: trajectories are flatter and more
compressed, consistent with lower submission volumes.
Team identifiers follow Table7.

Figure 15:Weekly submission activity heatmap.Each cell records the number ofFinishedsubmissions by a given
team (rows) during a given calendar week (columns).
Colour intensity encodes volume: white = zero submissions,
dark blue = highest activity.
Cell values are annotated directly; values≥5{\geq}5are printed in
white for contrast.
Two distinct engagement patterns are visible: sustained high-activity
teams (Team A, Team C) versus late-concentrated teams (Teams K, E).
A competition-wide deadline surge is visible in the final two weeks.
Team identifiers follow Table7.

Figure 16:Learning dynamics and submission reliability.(a)Cumulative best score as a function of submission number,
ordered chronologically within each team.
Each marker is one finished submission; lines connect consecutive
submissions per team.
Most teams plateau within five submissions; Team A is the notable
exception with continued improvement past submission 20.
Team H plateaus early despite 31 total submissions, illustrating a
diminishing-returns failure mode.(b)Submission success rate (Finished÷\divTotal attempted)
per team per track.
Circles (∘\circ) = Task Planning; squares (□\square) = Task Execution.
Task Planning achieves≥80%{\geq}80\%success rates for most teams;
Task Execution shows substantially higher variability.
Team identifiers follow Table7.

Figure 17:Top-rank stability under score reparameterization.Each cell shows the identity of the top-ranked team for a given
combination of execution weightα\alphaandt-matchscaling
factorss.
The officially top-ranked team is stable only within a narrow band around
the released configuration; the top-ranked team changes across large regions of
the parameter space.
Mean Kendallτ¯=0.61\bar{\tau}=0.61(SD=0.19\text{SD}=0.19) between the
official ranking and rankings under each alternative configuration,
indicating moderate robustness.

Figure 18:Distribution of public leaderboard scores.Scores cluster at a small set of discrete values arising from binary
per-scenario evaluation (∼9.09\sim\!9.09points per scenario with 11
scenarios total).
Four teams share the maximum planning-track score of 72.73, illustrating
that the public evaluation signal cannot discriminate between these teams
on the planning track; a second cluster appears at 63.64 points.
This saturation means the execution component is the decisive
discriminator for the top-four ranking positions.

Figure 19:Rank shifts between public and hidden evaluation.Each histogram shows the distribution of rank changesΔ​r=rhidden−rpublic\Delta r=r_{\text{hidden}}-r_{\text{public}}per team.
Planning track: Spearmanρ=0.62\rho=0.62(p=0.04p=0.04), moderate
significant alignment with maximum absolute shift of 6 positions.
Execution track: Spearmanρ=−0.13\rho=-0.13(p=0.71p=0.71), near-zero
non-significant alignment with maximum absolute shift of 8 positions,
indicating that public execution scores do not predict hidden performance.

Figure 20:Public vs. hidden score comparison per track.Planning (left panel) shows a recognisable positive relationship between
public and hidden scores.
Execution (right panel) shows scatter consistent with near-zero
correlation, confirming that public execution scores do not provide a
reliable proxy for hidden performance.
Shaded regions indicate the 95% confidence band around the regression
line where fitted.

Figure 21:Distribution of computational cost metrics across Phase 1
executions.All three metrics are right-skewed.
Token consumption: median 54K, mean 110K, CV=1.47=1.47, indicating a
small number of high-token executions dominate aggregate cost.
API call depth and wall-clock duration are similarly right-skewed but
with lower dispersion, reflecting structural constraints of the agent
architecture rather than per-token efficiency differences.

Figure 22:Cost comparison between Phase 1 (Development) and Phase 2
(Evaluation).Error bars denote one standard deviation.
Token consumption (p=0.82p=0.82) and wall-clock duration (p=0.27p=0.27) are
statistically indistinguishable, confirming comparable computational
demands across scenario sets.
API call depth differs significantly (p=0.004p=0.004): Phase 1 averages
11.5 calls vs. 10.0 in Phase 2, consistent with more exploratory
multi-step behaviour during development.

Figure 23:Mean execution cost per agent domain across all three metrics.Error bars denote one standard deviation.
WO is the most expensive domain across token consumption and API calls;
E2E has the highest wall-clock duration despite moderate token consumption,
exposing an orchestration latency cost invisible to token-based analysis.
TSFM is consistently the least expensive domain.

Figure 24:Execution cost comparison between single-agent and multi-agent
(E2E) partitions.Single-agent executions consume nearly twice the tokens of multi-agent
executions (121K vs. 63K,t=7.18t=7.18,p<0.001p<0.001), driven by the
dominance of token-intensive WO Decision Support scenarios in the
single-agent pool.
Multi-agent executions have higher wall-clock duration despite lower
token consumption, reflecting orchestration latency.

Figure 25:Mean token consumption per scenario ordered by difficulty.Left panel: Phase 1 (Development); right panel: Phase 2 (Evaluation).
Error bars denote one standard deviation across runs.
Colours indicate agent domain.
The 18-fold difficulty range between the hardest scenario (Q424, WO,
373K tokens) and the easiest (Q201, TSFM, 20K tokens) confirms extreme
difficulty heterogeneity.
Spearmanρ=0.89\rho=0.89(p<0.001p<0.001) between phase orderings confirms
that scenario difficulty is an intrinsic task property, not an artefact
of participant pool or utterance form.

Figure 26:Feature correlation heatmap showing relationships
between trajectory features and task success.

Figure 27:Tool entropy vs task success. Successful
executions exhibit lower entropy.

Figure 28:Number of steps vs task success. Successful
executions are shorter.

Figure 29:Token usage vs task success. Successful executions
use fewer tokens.

matched cluster pair betweenMiniLM(rows) andBGE(columns) atK=5K{=}5. Chance overlap under an independent random
assignment is≈1\approx 1medoid per pair. Track 2 (right) has

images.caption: Figure 32:Distribution of cluster sizes across failure-mode labels. Most clusters are small (size 1–3), while a few large clusters capture dominant failure patterns.
